questions.prompt: You're at the registrar's domain-management panel, about to toggle auto-renew off for a domain the client decided to drop. The panel shows 30 client domains in a scrollable list. What's the reflex?
choices: Click the toggle on the row you scrolled to; the panel knows which domain it's for.
Verify the row by reading the domain name twice before clicking.
Disable auto-renew on all 30 domains and re-enable the ones you want to keep.
Toggle auto-renew off then re-enable to test.
questions.explanation: The wrong-row click schedules the wrong domain for non-renewal, and the damage only appears at the renewal cycle months later. Position in the scroll isn't reliable; misalignment between the row label and the toggle is the documented failure mode. The 10-second pause to verify by name (not position) prevents the slow-motion failure that doesn't surface until much later.

questions.prompt: A security consultant recommends adding `CAA 0 issue "digicert.com"`. The client uses DigiCert for their wildcard. What's the cardinal-sin check?
choices: TTL: confirm the CAA TTL is appropriate.
Check what's currently issuing for the domain via Certificate Transparency logs.
Sign-off from the security consultant is enough.
Just publish; CA enforcement is loose.
questions.explanation: If other CAs are auto-issuing (Let's Encrypt via a managed platform, for example) and you publish CAA listing only DigiCert, those other issuances will fail silently at the next renewal. Cardinal sin 7. The consultant didn't necessarily check current issuance. CAA is enforced by every major CA; missing a current issuer means cert renewal fails weeks later, after the tech has forgotten the change. CT log search (`crt.sh/?q=example.com`) is the fast check.

questions.prompt: A registrar approval email arrives in the MSP's shared inbox: 'A transfer of `important-client.com` has been initiated. Click here to approve.' You don't recall authorising any transfer. What do you do?
choices: Click approve to keep the queue moving; the email is from the registrar.
Verify out-of-band: contact the client (via phone or the contact on file.
Forward the email to the client and ask them to handle it.
Ignore the email; if it was legitimate the client will follow up.
questions.explanation: Not by reply to the email) and confirm the transfer was authorised. If not, this is a hijacking attempt. Cardinal sin 9. Approving an unauthorised transfer-out lets a hijacker take the domain. Out-of-band confirmation through a known channel is the verification step. Forwarding lets the client be deceived too; ignoring is passive but slow.
